text:
Elegance, to me, lives somewhere between economy and music. A sentence feels elegant when it carries exactly what it must and nothing it merely could, when every word has earned its place and seems faintly surprised, on rereading, that any other word was ever considered. There is a pleasure in necessity, in the sense that the thing could not have been arranged otherwise without loss.

But economy alone is austere, and austerity is not elegance. The elegant sentence also has rhythm, a breath in it. It knows where to pause, where to let a clause open like a door and where to close it firmly. I find I trust sentences that vary their pace, that lengthen when the thought is patient and snap shut when the thought is sure. A well-placed short sentence after a long one feels like landing.

I am drawn, too, to sentences that hold two things at once, that turn a small hinge in the middle and arrive somewhere the beginning did not quite promise. The reversal need not be clever. It only needs to feel honest, as though the writer discovered the second half rather than engineered it. The cleverness that announces itself is the enemy of grace.

And there is something harder to name: a quality of attention. Elegant prose seems to have looked closely at its subject, to have resisted the easy abstraction in favor of the particular. A concrete noun in the right place can redeem a whole paragraph. Specificity is a kind of respect, both for the thing described and for the reader trusted to see it.

Perhaps what unites these is restraint that does not read as restraint, effort that has erased its own traces. The elegant sentence wears its labor lightly. It does not strain for effect, does not raise its voice, does not ask to be admired. It simply says the true thing in the shape the truth seemed to want, and then, with admirable tact, it stops.
